ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an effective and well-recognized strategy used in many delivery systems, such as polycation gene vectors, charge reversal refers to the alternation of vector surface charge from negative (in blood circulation) to positive (in the targeted tissue) in response to specific stimuli to simultaneously satisfy the requirements of biocompatibility and targeting. Although charge reversal vectors are intended to avoid interactions with blood in their application, no overall or systematic investigation has been carried out to verify the role of charge reversal in the blood compatibility. Herein, we comprehensively mapped the effects of a typical charge-reversible polycation gene vector based on pH-responsive 2,3-dimethylmaleic anhydride (DMMA)-modified polyethylenimine (PEI)/pDNA complex in terms of blood components, coagulation function, and immune response as compared to conventional PEGylated modification. The in vitro and in vivo results displayed that charge-reversal modification significantly improves the PEI/pDNA-induced abnormal effect on vascular endothelial cells, platelet activation, clotting factor activity, fibrinogen polymerization, blood coagulation process, and pro-inflammatory cytokine expression. Unexpectedly, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA induced the cytoskeleton impairment-mediated erythrocyte morphological alternation and complement activation even more than PEI/pDNA. Further, transcriptome sequencing demonstrated that the overexpression of pro-inflammatory cytokines was correlated with vector-induced differentially expressed gene number and mediated by inflammation-related signaling pathways (MAPK, NF-κB, Toll-like receptor, and JAK-STAT) activation. By comparison, charge-reversal modification improved the hemocompatibility to a greater extent than dose PEGylation except for erythrocyte rupture. Nevertheless, it is inferior to mPEG modification in terms of immunocompatibility. These findings provide comprehensive insights to understand the molecular mechanisms of the effects of charge reversal on blood components and their function and to provide valuable information for its potential applications from laboratory to clinic.

The seemingly revolutionary charge reversal strategy has been believed to possess stealth character with negative charge eluding interaction with blood components during circulation. However to date, no overall or systematic investigation has been carried out to verify the role of charge-reversal on the blood/immune compatibility, which impede their development from laboratory to bedside. Therefore, we comprehensively mapped the effects of a typical charge-reversible polycationic gene vector on blood components (vascular endothelial cell, platelet, clotting factors, fibrinogen, RBCs and coagulation function) and immune response (complement and pro-inflammatory cytokines) at cellular and molecular level in comparison to PEGylation modification. These findings help to elucidate the molecular mechanisms for the effects of charge-reversal on blood components and functions, and provide valuable information for the possible application in clinical settings.

ce:section.ce:para: Charge reversal is an effective, widely used strategy in many drug delivery systems to deliver cancer drug through the blood circulation [1–4]. The surface charge of charge-reversal vectors can be altered (usually from negative to positive) in response to specific stimuli, which allows the vector to maintain its stealth character (negative charge) during blood circulation to elude clearance. The vectors can then be transformed into positively charged forms to enhance their interaction with target cells, producing greater site-specific accumulation. Thus, charge-reversal strategy combines the advantage of stimuli response-mediated passive targeting [5–7] with biocompatibility for systematic delivery. Among various stimuli, tumor acidity-activated responsiveness is the most frequently used, as pH in the tumor microenvironment is more acidic (pHe ≈ 6.5) than that of blood (pH ≈ 7.4), and the pH values of endosomes and lysosomes are even lower (4.0–6.0) [8–10]. Recently, a universal molecular modification strategy was developed to construct pH-dependent charge-reversal delivery system by converting the primary amines of the vectors into carboxylic acid groups with neighboring amides using acid anhydride (such as 2,3-dimethylmaleic anhydride, DMMA). The β-carboxylic amide displays pH-dependent hydrolysis, subsequently achieving a negative-to-positive charge reversal under weak acid conditions [11,12]. For example, Wang’s group developed a DMMA-modified nanogel for promoted tumoral cell uptake and DOX delivery in the slightly acidic tumor extracellular environment. Moreover, while Gu’s group reported DMMA-modified dendritic lipopeptides that mimic viral architecture and possess tumor extracellular targeting by virtue of charge-reversible shielding. In addition, our group proposed an image-guided, cascade-responsive hybrid carbon dot modified with a DMMA-functionalized PEGylated shell. It exhibited effective tumor accumulation induced by the microenvironment-driven charge conversion and size shrinkage [11,13,14]. Although the seemingly revolutionary charge-reversal strategy may lead to prolonged circulation in the blood by minimizing nonspecific interactions with blood components, studies of the biocompatibility have been limited to cytotoxicity and hemolysis [14–16]. During circulation, charge-reversible vectors inevitably contact various blood components such as red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), platelets, plasma proteins, and complements. The compatibility of the underlying properties of charge-reversible vectors with the blood/immune components needs to be evaluated before their viability and safety in the circulation can be verified.

Polycationic gene delivery systems are a class of vectors that commonly use the charge-reversal strategy. To achieve potent transfection, polycationic gene vectors are usually formed with a strongly positively charged surface that encapsulates the negatively charged gene in a complex based on electrostatic interactions. The resulting interactions with blood components are nonspecific, as they are also negatively charged. Our previous studies found that typical polycationic gene carriers (i.e., polyethyleneimine, PEI, and poly(amidoamine), PAMAM) had a severe impact on the erythrocyte membrane structure, thereby inhibiting the blood coagulation process and altering fibrinogen conformation [17,18]. The positively charged polycationic gene vectors have also been reported to be easily captured by the reticuloendothelial system (RES) and eliminated by the mononuclear phagocytic system (MPS) [19–21]. In addition, the introduction of a polycationic vector into the blood may cause an immune response [22–24]. To avoid the above-mentioned side effects of a cationic polymer and improve the biocompatibility, a PEGylated modification can be used to shield the surface charge [21,25]. The charge-reversal strategy is an alternative that not only shields the surface charge but also passively targets the tumor tissue. The question arises as to whether or not this strategy can replace conventional PEGylated modifications in terms of hemocompatibility.

Despite the abundance of studies on charge-reversible polycations as gene vectors, three basic questions remain unanswered:
1)
On entering the circulation, can charge-reversal modification of the polycationic gene vector have a significant effect on blood components or coagulation functions, and if so, to what extent?
2)
Apart from any effect on the coagulation function, can charge-reversal modification positively or negatively influence the polycation-induced immune response (i.e., inflammation) in blood?
3)
Is charge reversal modification a superior strategy compared to the widely used PEGylation, in terms of blood compatibility?

To answer these questions, the branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) with molecular weight of 25 kDa (golden standard for gene delivery) was selected as the model of polycationic gene vector [26–28], and EGFP plasmid DNA was loaded as the model gene. Three groups were designed as follows (Scheme 1 a): 1) PEI/pDNA complex as the polycationic gene vector group, 2) DMMA-modified PEI/pDNA ((PEI/pDNA)-DMMA) as the charge-reversible gene vector group, and 3) mPEG-modified PEI/pDNA ((PEI/pDNA)-mPEG) as the PEGylated gene vector group. This work focuses specifically on using a comprehensive approach to assess the hemocompatibility of the above groups (Scheme 1b). First, the effects on blood components (i.e., RBCs, platelets, vascular endothelial cells, fibrinogen, and coagulation factors) and coagulation function were studied. In vitro measurements were made with human blood to mimic clinical condition. In addition, the effects on the immune system components in the blood (i.e., complement, monocytes, and their inflammatory effects) were investigated. The mechanism by which vectors affect monocytes was also assessed by high-throughput transcriptome sequencing. Finally, the vector-induced effects on blood coagulation and immune inflammation were verified in vivo. This work was intended to provide a thorough understanding of the charge-reversal vector’s blood compatibility and its molecular mechanisms to design delivery systems with better biosafety and for possible use in clinical settings.

Branched poly(ethylenimine) (PEI) with an average molecular weight of 25 kDa (PEI-25k) and fibrinogen were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). N-hydroxysuccinimide-modified polyethylene glycol (mPEG2k-NHS) was purchased from Xi’an Ruixi Biological Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanxi, China). DMMA was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China) and used as received.

Blood was collected from healthy volunteers in sodium citrate tubes with a blood/anticoagulant ratio of 9:1. RBCs were obtained by centrifugation at 1000g for 5 min to remove plasma and washed three times with PBS. Platelet-poor plasma (PPP) and platelet-rich plasma (PRP) were prepared by centrifuging the citrated whole blood at 3000g for 5 min and at 800g for 5 min, respectively. Reagents for conventional coagulation assays APTT and PT were provided by the First Affiliated Hospital of Jinan University. Female Wistar rats (200 ± 20 g) were purchased from Jinan Pengyue Experimental Animal Breeding Co., Ltd. All experiments involving live animals or human blood were conducted in accordance with the laws and guidelines of Jinan University and were approved by the Ethics Committee of Jinan University.

The PEI-25 k solution (5 mg/mL) was mixed with the EGFP plasmid at an optimal ratio for half an hour to obtain a PEI/pDNA complex. For (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA preparation, DMMA was added to the PEI/pDNA solution and stirred for 6 h at room temperature. The pH value was maintained at 8–9 during the reaction. For (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG preparation, a certain amount of mPEG2k-NHS was added to the PEI/pDNA solution (pH ∼7.0) to ensure a similar grafting ratio relative to DMMA modification. After that, the two product solutions were dialyzed against water, which had been adjusted to pH 7.4 for 24 h using a dialysis membrane and then lyophilized.

1H NMR spectra of PEI, PEI-DMMA and PEI-mPEG were obtained with a 300 MHz NMR spectrometer (AVANCEIII 300 MHz, Bruker, Germany), and D2O was used as the solvent. The zeta potential at different pH values (pH 7.4, pH 6.5, and pH 5.0) and the size of the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA complex were measured with a Malvern Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, United Kingdom). Moreover, the morphology of the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA complexes was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010HR, Japan). PEI/pDNA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG complexes served as the control groups. The concentration of all groups was set at 0.5 mg/mL.

Agarose gel electrophoresis was performed as follows: 0.7 g of agarose was added to 70 mL of TAE buffer, and the solution was heated for 2 min in a microwave oven. GoldView-II dye (3 μL) was subsequently added to the agarose solution, and the mixture was poured into a plastic mold and cooled for 2 h. Then, 2 μL of the loading buffer was mixed with the reaction solution containing PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, and the mixtures were added to the gel wells (total volume of 20 μL). Naked pDNA was used as the control. The samples were electrophoresed at 150 V for 15 min. The migration of DNA was visualized and photographed under a UV lamp using a Bio-Rad Gel Doc XR+ System.

The trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid (TNBSA) method is commonly used to quantify the amount of primary amines [29]. To calculate the degree of substitution of DMMA or mPEG on PEI/pDNA, the TNBSA method was employed. Specifically, equimolar amounts of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG were dissolved in 500 μL of 0.1 M NaHCO3 (final concentration of the samples was 4 nmol/mL), and 250 μL of 0.01% TNBSA solution was added to it. The solutions were mixed and then incubated for 2 h at 37 °C in the dark. Then, 250 μL of 10% SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate) and 125 μL of 1 M HCl were added to terminate the reaction. Finally, the absorption spectra of samples were measured in the range of 300–450 nm with an ultraviolet spectrophotometer [30]. Glycine (concentration range 2–20 μg/mL) was used to plot the standard curve. All samples were set in triplicates.

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) (Sigma-Aldrich, St Louis, USA), supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin under 5% CO2 at 37 °C. Human monocytic leukemia cell line (THP-1) cells were cultured in the same environment in Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI) 1640 medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake analysis by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: FITC was dissolved in DMSO in the dark and added dropwise to the PEI solution under ice bath. (The mass ratio of PEI to FITC was 100:1 and the volume ratio was 3:1.) Then the reaction mixture was stirred for 15 h at room temperature. The mixture was dialyzed in the dark for 3 days until the dialysate had no color and lyophilized to obtain FITC-PEI-25k. The FITC-labeled (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG were prepared with the same batch of FITC-labeled PEI-25k as described. HUVEC suspension (500 μL) at a density of 1 × 105/mL was seeded into a 24-well plate for overnight incubation. The medium was then replaced with complete DMEM containing FITC-labeled samples and cultured for a further 3 h. After washing three times with PBS and trypsinized, the cell suspension was collected for flow cytometry analysis to detect endocytosis percentage.

A colorimetric cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) (Dojindo Laboratories, Kyoto, Japan) was used to evaluate the toxicity of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG on HUVECs. Specifically, 100 μL of HUVEC suspension was seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with the same volume of fresh medium containing samples at concentrations of 1–500 μg/mL for another 24 h. The medium was then removed, and the microplate was washed three times with PBS; 10% CCK-8 complete medium was added into all wells. OD values were measured at 450 nm with a microplate reader within 1–4 h. Cells without treatment were used as normal controls. Cell viability was calculated as follows:
Cell
viability =
OD sample
- OD
blank
OD standard
- OD
blank

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative Real-Time PCR (qRT-PCR) for detecting the expression of TF and t-PA mRNA in HUVECs
ce:section.ce:para: HUVECs were seeded at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well in 24-well plates and incubated for 24 h. The culture medium was then replaced with sample solutions with PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG at a concentration of 10 μg/mL, and the plates were incubated for another 24 h. HUVECs without treatment were used as the negative control. Total RNA was isolated using TRIzol (Thermo Fisher Scientific) according to the manufacturer's protocol, and real-time PCR was carried out using a StepOnePlus RT-qPCR system (ABI, USA) to detect the expression level of TF and t-PA mRNA. Relative quantities of gene transcripts were calculated by the 2−ΔΔCT method.

RBCs were incubated with PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG solutions of various concentrations at 37 °C for 15 min, and PBS-treated RBCs served as a control. After incubation, the RBC suspension was centrifuged and washed with PBS to remove the free sample material, and 1 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde solution was add to fix the RBCs for 12 h. Of the fixed RBC suspensions, 20 μL was moved onto the glass slides, dehydrated in an ascending series of ethanol (70%, 85%, 95%, and 100%), and finally dried at room temperature. After drying, the RBCs were coated with gold and observed with a scanning electron microscope (Philips XL-30, Holland).

RBCs were diluted with PBS to 16% cell suspension, and 50 μL of this solution was added to 1 mL of the three groups at different concentrations and then incubated at 37 °C. At predetermined incubation times (1 h, 3 h, 6 h, 9 h, and 12 h), the supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 1000g for 5 min and added to a 96-well plate. OD values were measured at 540 nm with a microplate reader (Multiskan MK3, Thermo Fisher Scientific). RBCs were incubated with PBS (0% lysis) and water (100% lysis) as the negative and positive controls, respectively. The hemolysis rate was calculated as
hemolysis
rate =
OD sample
-
OD PBS
OD water
-
OD PBS

RBCs were incubated with PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG solution at a concentration of 0.05 mg/mL for 15 min at 37 °C, with a PBS group serving as the negative control. The vector-treated RBCs were washed with PBS to remove excess samples and then fixed by adding 4% paraformaldehyde for 12 h. After washing the fixed RBCs with PBS, 0.1% Triton-X penetrating buffer was added for 10 s, then immediately washed with PBS, and finally stained with FITC-conjugated phalloidin at room temperature for 20 min in the dark to reveal F-actin on the RBC skeleton. Finally, the cytoskeletal structure of treated RBCs was observed under a confocal laser-scanning microscope.

Platelet activation was determined by measuring the expression of the platelet activation marker CD62P (P-Selectin) with a flow cytometer (Calibur, BD Biosciences, USA). Briefly, 90 μL of PRP was mixed with 10 μL of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG for 1 h at 37 °C. Ten microliters of the mixture was diluted with 90 μL of N-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine-N′-(2-ethanesulfonic acid) (HEPES) buffer, and 5 μL of anti-CD62P-PE (eBioscience Inc., San Diego, USA) was then added to the mixture, which was then incubated in the dark for 15 min. PBS was used as a negative control, while human thrombin was used as a positive control, and FITC-conjugated goat anti-mouse IgG polyclonal antibodies (Abcam) were used as a nonspecific binding control. Each sample was paralleled three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activated partial thromboplastic time (APTT) and prothrombin time (PT) assays
ce:section.ce:para: The extrinsic and intrinsic pathways of coagulation were determined by measuring the APTT and PT in PPP. Measurements were made with an SF-8000 automatic coagulation analyzer (Beijing Succeeder Company, Beijing, China). Specifically, 180 μL of PPP was mixed with 20 μL of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG to obtain final concentrations of 0.01, 0.1, and 1 mg/mL; PBS was used as a normal control. The mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 15 min before making the measurement. Each group was performed in triplicate.

To investigate the interaction between the carrier/gene complexes and fibrinogen, an isothermal titration calorimeter (ITC) was performed with an Affinity ITC (TA Instruments, USA). Titrations were performed by injecting 100 μL of fibrinogen solution (5 mg/mL) into the sample cell containing 350 μL of 0.04 μmol/mL of vector/gene complex at 37 °C. Specifically, 20 drops of fibrinogen solution (2.5 μL each) was consecutively titrated into the cell at a stirring speed of 250 rpm; the titration time intervals were 200 s to allow for a sufficient equilibrium reaction. The PBS solution was titrated with an equal amount of fibrinogen solution as a background titration to subtract the heat released by the dilution. The data were analyzed using NanoAnalyze software, and the cumulative calories released were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology analysis of fibrin clot by SEM
ce:section.ce:para: The PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG solution was mixed with fibrinogen solution in equal volume, and 20 μL of 40 mM CaCl2 and 15 μL of human thrombin (5 NIHU (National Institutes of Health unit)/mL) were added. These mixtures were then incubated at 37 °C for 1 h to allow the formation of fibrin clot. A mixture of PBS and fibrinogen solution served as a normal control. After incubation, each sample was fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 2 h and then washed three times with PBS. The clot was lyophilized and stuck onto a conductive adhesive. Clot specimens were sprayed with gold, and their morphology was observed with a scanning electron microscope (Philips XL-30, Holland).

A turbidity analysis was performed to investigate the polymerization of fibrin in the presence of PEI/pDNA, PEI/pDNA-DMMA, or PEI/pDNA-mPEG. Briefly, human fibrinogen solution was mixed with equal volume of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG in PBS with a final concentration at 0.1 mg/mL, and the mixture was preincubated at 37 °C for 10 min. The pure fibrinogen solution in PBS was used as a normal control. Mixtures were then added to a 96-well plate, and the reaction was initiated by the addition of 20 μL CaCl2 (40 mM) and 15 μL human thrombin (5 NIHU/mL). The aggregation curve within one hour was measured at 37 °C with a microplate reader at 405 nm. Each sample was set in triplicate. The aggregation rate of fibrin was determined by the changes in OD with time.

The vector/gene complexes (PEI/pDNA, PEI/pDNA-DMMA, or PEI/pDNA-mPEG) were mixed with whole blood at a 1:9 (volume) ratio for one minute in a kaolin-containing tube for the coagulation study using a thromboelastograph (TEG) hemostasis system (TEG-5000, Haemoscope Corporation) at 37 °C. PBS was used as a negative control. Then, 20 μL of CaCl2 solution (0.2 M) was added to 340 μL of the blood/complex mixture in a TEG test cup. The blood clotting process was immediately recorded.

To assess complement activation induced by PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG in human blood, the amount of the activated peptide C3a (cleaved complement component C3) was detected using a commercial C3a enzyme immunoassay kit (eBioscience Inc., San Diego, USA). Briefly, 100 μL of PPP was mixed with 100 μL of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG to obtain the final concentrations of 0.01, 0.1, and 1 mg/mL and then incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The incubated sample was gradient-diluted 1000 times, and the test was conducted according to the protocol of the kit.

In vitro pro-inflammatory secretion was estimated in THP-1 cell line and human plasma and quantitatively detected by ELISA. On the one hand, THP-1 cells were incubated with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG at a density of 2 × 105 cells/well for 24 h, and the cell supernatant was harvested. On the other hand, whole blood was incubated with an equal volume of the three groups (final concentration of 0.1 mg/mL) for 4 h at 37 °C, and the cytokine-containing plasma was collected by centrifugation. Both the collected cell supernatant and the plasma were subjected to detection of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α) using commercial human cytokine ELISA kit (NEOBIOSCIENCE, China.) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA sequencing analysis of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) and signaling pathways
ce:section.ce:para: THP-1 cells were incubated with 10 μg/mL of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG solution for 24 h, and untreated cells served as controls. The cells were then washed three times with PBS, and the total RNA was isolated with TRIzol. The RNA sequencing was outsourced to Chi Biotech Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China). The extracted RNA was used to construct a library, and each sample was sequenced. The reads obtained by sequencing each sample were then aligned with a reference sequence (reference library: HUMAN RefSeq-RNA hg19) using FANse3 ultra-high-precision sequence alignment algorithm [31] and RPKM (Reads Per Kilo bases per Million reads) was calculated to obtain reliable gene expression levels. Correlation of gene expression levels between samples was expressed using the Pearson correlation coefficient (R) and plotted as a heatmap. The differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were then screened using the edgeR assay [32]. The screening threshold for the DEGs in this analysis was set to: |log2 (FoldChange)| > 1 and p value <0.05. Finally, gene ontology (GO) enrichment and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analysis for DEGs were performed to understand its specific biological process (BPs) (http://go.princeton.edu/cgi-bin/GOTermFinder) and (http://www.genome.jp/).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo assessment on coagulation Function, blood Routine, and cytokine secretion
ce:section.ce:para: Wistar rats (200 ± 20 g) were randomly divided into five groups (five in each group). PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG complexes, dissolved in 0.9% physiological saline, was injected into the rats through the tail vein (5 mg/kg per rat). Saline (0.9%) was used as a negative control, and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (7.5 mg/kg per rat) was used as a positive control for the inflammatory response. After 24 h, these rats were euthanized, and the whole blood was collected for routine blood analysis, TEG testing, and cytokine detection in serum.

The in vivo TEG detection process is the same as the in vitro TEG detection procedure. The four reference range values (R, K, α, and MA) of the rats were established by test values of multiple saline groups.

After intravenous administration of the vector/gene complexes, rat blood was collected in anticoagulation tubes. Routine blood testing was performed with an automatic blood cell analyzer (BC-2800 Vet, Shenzhen Mindray Bio-Medical Electronics Co., Ltd., China).

For the detection of pro-inflammatory cytokines in vivo, rats were administered three complexes for 24 h through the tail vein, and the serum was separated. The expression of the cytokines IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α in the serum was measured using rat IL-1β, rat IL-6, and rat TNF-α ELISA kit from MultiSciences (Lianke Biotech Co., Ltd., China).
All data are presented as mean ± SD. Student’s t-test was performed to calculate the statistical differences (significant for *p < 0.05, and very significant for **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of charge-reversible vector
ce:section.ce:para: As a typical polycationic gene vector, PEI-25k was loaded with plasmid DNA and its charge-reversible and PEGylated derivatives were denoted as PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG. The procedure for preparing these groups is shown in Scheme 1a. Specifically, the PEI/pDNA complex formed by electrostatic interaction was used as a polycationic gene vector control group in this work. The charge-reversible group (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA was prepared by grafting the DMMA onto the outer amine groups of the PEI/pDNA complex by Michael addition reaction. The PEGylated gene vector group (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG was obtained by grafting N-hydroxylsuccinimide-terminated mPEG (2000 Da) onto PEI/pDNA. To achieve comparable experiment results, similar grafting ratios of DMMA and mPEG were obtained by adjusting feed ratios.

1H NMR characterization was done to confirm the successful grafting of DMMA and mPEG onto PEI. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the signals at δ 2.53–2.61 ppm (red line) belong to the proton peak of PEI, and the signals at δ 1.66–1.79 ppm (green line) belong to the proton peak of the two methyl groups in DMMA. The strong signal at δ 3.53 ppm (blue line) represents the methylene proton peak in PEG, while the signal at δ 3.22 ppm represents the proton peak of the terminal methoxy group in the mPEG chain. Both DMMA and mPEG can be inferred to have been conjugated to PEI to form PEI-DMMA and PEI-mPEG, respectively. The degree of substitution of DMMA and mPEG was approximately 38 ± 5% and 30 ± 5%, respectively, as determined by the TNBSA method (Fig. S1).

To verify nucleic acid leakage during DMMA or mPEG modifications, the reaction solutions were subjected to gel electrophoresis assay, and the results are shown in Fig. 1b. No fluorescent bands can be observed in the three complexes groups, demonstrating that almost no free plasmids exist in the solution. This indicates that DMMA or mPEG modification does not result in nucleic acid leakage from PEI/pDNA.

The charge-reversal performance of (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA was verified by measuring the zeta potential at different pH values, with comparisons to those of PEI/pDNA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG. Fig. 1c shows that the zeta potential of (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA was reversed from −18.0 ± 1.2 mV (pH 7.4) to +13.3 ± 1.5 mV (pH 6.5) and then to +16.7 ± 2.0 mV (pH 5.0) with the change in pH, while the zeta potentials of PEI/pDNA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG showed no apparent charge inversions, remaining at approximately +16.8 ± 1.2 mV and +5.7 ± 0.8 mV, respectively. These results indicate that both DMMA and mPEG modifications could effectively shield the high positive charge of PEI/pDNA, while the DMMA modification can lower the charge to a negative value and the mPEG modification only reduced the surface charge to a near-neutral value. In addition, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA could reverse its charge, maintaining a negative value at the pH of blood and changing its charge to a positive value after reaching a weak acidic environment such as the tumor tissue.

The size and morphology of three vector/gene complexes were examined with dynamic light scattering (DLS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1d and e). The DLS results show that the hydrodynamic diameters of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG in aqueous solution were 208 ± 13 nm, 216 ± 7 nm, and 238 ± 17 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d and Table S1). The TEM results (Fig. 1e) show that the three complexes all reveal irregular sphere with a size range from 50 to 200 nm in the dry state. The difference in size between the TEM and DLS measurements was related to the hydration state of complexes. Both results demonstrate that DMMA or mPEG modification did not change the complex size as expected, indicating that (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG kept the good ability to condense the plasmids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect on human vascular endothelial cells
ce:section.ce:para: Vascular endothelial cells play an important role in vasodilation, platelet adhesion, leukocyte interactions, and secretion of soluble or presentation of membrane-bound pro-coagulant and anticoagulant factors [33]. Therefore, HUVECs were used to study the effects of (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA complex on toxicity, endocytosis, and coagulation factor-related gene expression in endothelial cells.

Cytotoxicity of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG on HUVECs was first measured by the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the cell viability for all three groups was evidently concentration dependent, and the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG groups exhibited much higher cell viability than the PEI/pDNA group as the concentration was increased. When the concentration reached 50 μg/mL, cell viability of the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group was more than 80%, the cell viability of the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group was approximately 40%, and the cell viability of the PEI/pDNA group was reduced to less than 20%. These results indicate that both DMMA and mPEG can shield the positive charge on PEI and effectively improve the cell viability. Moreover, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA appeared to be more cytocompatible than (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, suggesting that the cytotoxicity of the polycation/gene complex depended on the surface charge. Thus, charge-reversible DMMA modification appears to prevent interactions of polycationic gene vector with vascular endothelial cells.

We then examined the endocytosis of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG by HUVECs by flow cytometry at the pH value of blood. As shown in Fig. 2b, the negatively charged (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA had the lowest rate of endocytosis compared to the positively charged PEI/pDNA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG groups. The PEI/pDNA also showed a significantly higher rate than the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, indicating that the endocytosis rate was closely correlated to the surface charge of the vector. This confirmed that effective shielding of the cationic surface charge can reduce cell uptake, which is consistent with previous reports [16].

Once foreign invaders enter the bloodstream, they inevitably interact with vascular endothelial cells and are endocytosed, which may affect the expression of coagulation-associated factors in the endothelial cells, and further influence the coagulation function. Tissue factor (TF) and tissue-type plasminogen activator (t-PA) are key factors involved in regulating blood coagulation [34]. TF is the main coagulating substance that can induce the activation of coagulation factor VII under the action of Ca2+. It then initiates exogenous coagulation, accelerating the formation of thrombus; although t-PA can selectively activate plasminogen in the thrombus when a blood vessel is damaged or blocked, the degradation of fibrin and several coagulation factors restores smooth blood vessels.

Therefore, the mRNA expression of TF and t-PA in HUVECs was detected by quantitative real-time PCR assay, and the results are shown in Fig. 2c. PEI/pDNA highly promoted the expression of TF and strongly inhibited the expression of t-PA, indicating that PEI/pDNA, when used in a typical gene delivery system, might increase the risk of thrombus formation owing to the higher rate of HUVEC endocytosis. (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG had no significant effect on TF expression but slightly promoted t-PA expression. These results suggest that both DMMA and mPEG modification mitigated the undesirable alterations to coagulation factor-associated gene expression induced by PEI/pDNA in HUVECs. From the above results, DMMA-based charge-reversal and PEGylation were both effective strategies for preventing nonspecific endocytosis of the polycationic gene vector into vascular endothelial cells, avoiding vascular endothelial impairment, and mitigating the expression of abnormal coagulation factors. In particular, DMMA-based charge-reversal had reduced interactions with HUVECs compared to those of PEGylation owing to the charge repulsion with cell membranes.

RBCs are unavoidable targets for any blood delivery system owing to their high abundance (nearly 40–50% by volume) and have important functions in transporting gases and nutrients in the body [35]. Therefore, they can be used as a classic model for studying the interactions between mammalian cell membranes and foreign biomedical materials [18,36], and they have been widely used in hemocompatibility studies. In this work, the effects of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG have been evaluated in terms of human erythrocyte morphology, lysis, and membrane skeletal structure to assess whether or not charge-reversal modification can improve the interactions between polycation carriers and RBCs.

The morphology of RBCs treated with PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG solution at different concentrations was observed by SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 a. It was found that PBS-treated RBCs had a normal biconcave shape with a smooth cell surface, while vector-treated RBCs revealed the morphological alterations in a concentration-dependent manner ranging from 0.01 mg/mL to 0.1 mg/mL. Specifically, RBCs treated with the positively charged PEI/pDNA or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG appeared gathered together at concentrations above 0.05 or 0.1 mg/mL, respectively. PEI/pDNA had a more apparent effect on the RBC aggregation than (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, which was likely caused by its higher charge density. The RBC aggregation induced by electrostatic interaction in the presence of PEI was accorded with a previous report [17]. Unexpectedly, the negatively charged (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA solution appeared to have the greatest effect on RBC morphology, even at 0.01 mg/mL. As depicted in Fig. 3a, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA-induced protrusions on the RBCs tend to be stretched, which led to cell shrinkage. In particularly, at 0.1 mg/mL, all RBCs had lost their normal sharp and seemed to be ruptured. It is reported that the interactions between nanomaterials and RBCs include electrostatic interaction, hydrophobic interaction, hydrogen bonding, van der Waals force, etc. [36]. Therefore, the severe RBC morphological alteration caused by the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA may be attributed to the hydrophobic interaction between the surface hydrophobic methyl structure of the DMMA and the lipid bilayer of the RBCs.

Additionally, we further examined the membrane integrity of vector-treated RBCs with the hemolysis test, which is commonly used to assess the biosafety of various blood-contacting materials. The RBC hemolysis rate caused by these three vector groups was concentration- and time-dependent (Fig. 3b). Under different concentrations, the hemolysis rate of the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group was much higher than that of the other two groups, reaching almost 70% in one hour at 0.1 mg/mL. For the other two groups, mPEG-modified polycation had the lower hemolysis rate, suggesting that the mPEG modification could shield the surface charge of polycation and improve the compatibility. DMMA modification, however, caused severe membrane disturbances and RBC hemolysis, possibly because of hydrophobic methyl groups on DMMA inserting into the erythrocyte membrane. This result was also supported by the findings of the RBC morphology experiment.

To further elucidate the mechanism of how DMMA disturbs the RBC membrane, the RBC cytoskeleton was stained for F-actin and then viewed by confocal microscopy. The key structural component of the RBC cytoskeleton is the actin oligomer, whose ligation complex consists of short actin filaments (F-actin precursor) that can be bound with fluorescence-labeled phalloidin [37]. Fig. 3c shows fluorescence images of the RBC cytoskeleton. PBS-treated RBCs maintained their intact circular cytoskeletal structure. However, the RBC cytoskeleton treated with PEI/pDNA has obviously deformations with blunt protrusions and aggregates that appear as clusters, which is consistent with the SEM findings. With regard to RBCs treated with (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, most of the cytoskeleton maintains a nearly circular shape, although some cells lose their circular shape and display blunt protrusions on their membranes. As expected, the RBC cytoskeleton treated with (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA is dramatically deformed and shrunken, even to the point of rupturing into fragments. This may explain why (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA induced hemolysis in most RBCs, as the RBC cytoskeleton was likely damaged by the methyl groups on DMMA through hydrophobic interactions. The above observations demonstrate that the hydrophobic interactions induced by DMMA have an even stronger effect on RBC membranes than the electrostatic interactions of PEI. Because the charge-reversal range of the material is related to the substitution degree of DMMA, which is positively correlated with its hydrophobic effect. The higher substitution degree of DMMA on PEI/pDNA contributes to stronger hydrophobic interaction and more negatively charged carboxyl group of the vector. From these results, the DMMA-modified polycation gene vector was able to affect the cytoskeleton of the RBCs through hydrophobic interaction, thereby causing the morphological alternation of RBCs and leading to hemolysis.

Platelet activation is an important step in the clotting cascade, and abnormal platelet activation mediated by foreign nanomaterials may cause thrombosis in the body. CD62P (P-selectin) is a glycoprotein present in platelet alpha-granules translocated to the plasma membrane of platelets during platelet activation. Therefore, the expression of CD62P on the platelet surface is a biomarker for platelet activation [18]. In our study, CD62P expression in activated platelets was induced by PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . CD62P expression of activated platelets by all three vectors occurred in a concentration-dependent manner. Specifically, at a concentration of 0.01 mg/mL, only PEI/pDNA activated platelets compared with PBS control. When the concentration was increased to 0.1 mg/mL, only (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA showed slight activation of platelets, whereas both PEI/pDNA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG significantly activated platelets. When the concentration of vectors reached 1 mg/mL, platelets were significantly activated in all three groups. From these results, PEI/pDNA caused the highest platelet activation, while (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG exhibited more significant platelet activation than (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA. Generally, mPEG modification effectively prevents polycation-induced platelet activation, and it has been well reported before. However, only partial amino groups on PEI were substituted by mPEG to maintain the pDNA-binding capability. Hence, a proportion of amino groups still exist on the surface of (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, which exhibit a slight positively charged (+5.7 mV), inducing higher platelet activation than the negatively charged (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group. It could be concluded that the platelet activation level seems to be closely related to the surface charge of the polycation gene vector; the higher the positively charged surface of vector, the greater is the platelet activation. Therefore, either mPEG or DMMA modification could decrease PEI-induced platelet activation, whereas charge-reversible DMMA modification was better able to prevent platelet activation owing to the negatively charged surface.

APTT and PT are conventional clinical tests of coagulation and can be used to measure the effect of charge-reversible polycationic vector on the activity of coagulation factors. The blood coagulation cascade includes three pathways: intrinsic, extrinsic, and common pathways [36]. The APTT represents the level of the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways. The PT represents the level of extrinsic and common coagulation pathways. As shown in Fig. 5 , Overall, PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG can all prolong the APTT and PT values in a concentration-dependent manner, and the groups have greater impact on APTT than PT, which suggests that the vectors have a greater influence on the coagulation factors in plasma related to the intrinsic coagulation pathway than the extrinsic coagulation pathway. It was reported that PEIs could increase intrinsic coagulation pathway-related APTT value, yet had little effect on the extrinsic route of blood coagulation [17]. Therefore, the different effect on APTT and PT should attribute to the PEI-induced pathway-selective impact. Specifically, at the concentration of 0.01 mg/mL, APTT and PT values did not change obviously except for a slightly prolonged APTT caused by PEI/pDNA compared to the PBS control. When the concentration was increased to 0.1 mg/mL, PEI/pDNA could significantly prolong clotting time, particularly for APTT (>140 s). However, a much lower clotting time was seen in the presence of (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, particularly for PT (no significant different). As the concentration reached 1 mg/mL, both APTT and PT values were highly prolonged in all the three groups. It was worth mentioning that the PEI/pDNA group caused the most remarkable abnormal APTT and PT values, while (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA had the least effect. The increases in APTT and PT may be attributed to the electrostatic adsorption of the negatively charged plasma components (such as clotting factors and/or fibrinogen) on the positively charged polycation. These three vector-induced impacts on plasma coagulation time (APTT and PT) were likely to be associated with surface charge properties, as was the case for platelet activation. Similarly, charge-reversible DMMA modification led to greater amelioration of the PEI-induced abnormal APTT and PT prolongation and beyond that of PEGylation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects on fibrinogen and fibrin polymerization
ce:section.ce:para: Fibrinogen, a negatively charged protein existing in plasma, is one of the major components of coagulation. Under the action of thrombin, fibrinogen is cleaved into fibrin monomer and small fibrin polypeptide, and then it polymerizes and cross-links into highly insoluble fibrin multimer under the action of calcium ions [38]. In this work, we studied the effect of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG solutions on fibrinogen by ITC. ITC is an effective method to assess interactions between polymer and protein [39]. It can provide information of the thermodynamics, binding constants, and affinity of protein–polymer interactions. To investigate the interactions between PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG complex with fibrinogen, Fig. 6 a shows the raw ITC data (left) and the cumulative heat profile (right) for fibrinogen adsorption onto vectors. In Fig. 6a (left), the heat profile of the fibrinogen solution added to PBS was almost a straight line, indicating that the heat generated by the dilution was negligible. The total binding heat released from the PEI/pDNA–fibrinogen interaction was 210 μcal, while the total binding heat released from equimolar amounts of (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG was 78 μcal and 153 μcal, respectively. This indicated that PEI/pDNA exhibited the highest binding affinity for fibrinogen through a strong electrostatic interaction, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG had a reduced binding to fibrinogen owing to available binding sites being shielding by the mPEG chain. Moreover, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA showed the weakest binding affinity to fibrinogen. Apart from the decrease in binding sites because of DMMA grafting, the electrostatic repulsion derived from carboxyl group on DMMA also helped to prevent tight binding between (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and fibrinogen. According to the cumulative heat kinetics for the binding process of fibrinogen to the three vectors, the heat profile of PEI/pDNA to fibrinogen showed a rapid increase and quickly reached equilibrium, while the heat profile of (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG increased more slowly than PEI/pDNA but faster than (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, which did not reach equilibrium during the test. It indicated that the binding rate for the polycationic gene vector and fibrinogen also depended on the electrostatic binding affinity. This further demonstrated that the charge-reversible DMMA modification can significantly prevent the binding of polycation onto fibrinogen and presented better fibrinogen adsorption resistance than PEGylated modification.

Representative micrographs of fibrin clots formed by protofibril assembly were captured by SEM in the presence of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG. As shown in Fig. 6b, under the action of thrombin and calcium ion, fibrinogen in PBS ultimately formed a normal fibrin clot network structure with interlacing branches and relatively uniform porosity. In the presence of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, the morphology of the fibrin clots had altered appearances. Among them, in the presence of PEI/pDNA, the fibrin clots exhibited the most obvious alternation that the fibers were almost entangled to form abnormal aggregation of protofibrils with significantly increased aggregation density and thickened fiber diameter. Abnormal protofibril aggregations were similarly observed in the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group, although the aggregation density was less than that of the PEI/pDNA group. For the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group, the formed three-dimensional clots were not considerably different from those of the PBS control and no obvious aggregation could be found, but there was slight fibrin breakage detected in the micrograph. The morphological alterations in fibrin clot formation induced by these three vectors may also be due to their surface charge and hydrophobic properties. DMMA modification had been proved to be effective for alleviating the impact of PEI on fibrin clot formation. Previous studies have shown that fibrinogen can adsorb onto positively charged surfaces and polycations can alter clot structure by nonspecific binding [40]. The above morphological result was also supported by the ITC data.

To further verify that DMMA modification can significantly improve the effect of a polycationic gene carrier on fibrin clot formation, the kinetics of fibrin polymerization were examined with turbidity monitoring of optical density (OD) measurements initiated by thrombin and calcium ions in the presence of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG. Changes in turbidity are well correlated with overall fibrin polymerization, where the maximum rate of OD change is related to the aggregation of protofibril, and the final turbidity reflects the number and size of the fibrils. The fibrin polymerization plot and kinetics parameters are shown in Fig. 6c. The introduction of polycation/gene complexes caused a rapid increase in turbidity (OD), and the final OD values were significantly higher than those of the PBS control. As shown in the table of Fig. 6c, the final OD values in the presence of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, and (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA were 1.33, 1.26, and 1.25, respectively. The final increase in turbidity may be explained by the electrostatic interaction-induced growth in fibril size, which was seen in the previous findings for morphology. In addition, the aggregation rates of the protofibril were also affected by PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG, and (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, which were approximately 2.51, 1.43, and 1.40 times greater than that of the PBS group, respectively, based on changes in OD. This result implied that polycations can accelerate fibrin monomer lateral aggregation, whereas DMMA and mPEG modification attenuated this effect.

Based on ITC, morphological observations, and turbidity analysis for fibrin polymerization, the above findings indicate that the strong interaction between PEI and fibrinogen led the fibrin clots to form abnormally, with greater aggregation density and thicker fibrin size. The mPEG or DMMA modification can reduce the binding sites for fibrin on the polycations and alleviate the abnormal aggregation. In addition to shielding the binding sites, DMMA modification, because of its negative charge, also exhibits electrostatic repulsion to fibrin and reduces the effects of fibrin polymerization. As reported previously, polycationic protamine altered the structure (thicker) of fibrin to increase the turbidity (OD value) through nonspecific binding effects and impaired thrombin production [40–42]. Other than the strong hydrophobic interaction between (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and RBCs, the hydrophobic effect in the DMMA-modified materials did not reflect in the interaction with fibrinogen. Because the fibrinogen with highly amphiphilic nature is able to form a configuration exposing the hydrophilic domain outside and packaging the hydrophobic domain inside in the aqueous buffer [43]. Thus, the short hydrophobic methyl groups of DMMA can barely insert into the hydrophobic domain of fibrinogen owing to its thick hydrophilic domain around it. Therefore, the alleviated binding after DMMA modification may be due to more dominant influence of the electrostatic interaction over hydrophobic effect between (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and fibrinogen.

The blood coagulation process involves multiple components such as coagulation factors, fibrinogen, and platelets. TEG records the strength of the blood clot as a function of time during the dynamic coagulation process (Fig. 7 a). It comprehensively reflects the impact of nanomaterials delivered intravenously on the whole coagulation process. Therefore, the TEG test has become a recognized blood biochemical indicator in clinical and laboratory studies and mainly provides the following four parameters: 1) reaction time R, indicating the time from initiating the test to the initial fibrin formation; 2) clotting time K, representing the kinetics of blood clotting; 3) angle α, indicating the rate of clot polymerization; and 4) MA, representing the maximum clot strength. Among them, the R value reflects the activity of the coagulation factor, the K value and α angle reflect the activity of fibrinogen polymerization, and the MA value reflects the activity of platelet aggregation.

The influence of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG on whole-blood coagulation was studied with a TEG analyzer. Representative TEG traces for each concentration of vectors are shown in Fig. 7b, and the four principle TEG parameters are listed in Table 1 . From Fig. 7b, the TEG traces had been altered in the presence of the three groups of vectors in a concentration-dependent manner from 0.001 mg/mL to 1 mg/mL as compared to the PBS control. Of the three vectors, PEI/pDNA complex had the greatest impact on the TEG trace, while (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG had similar trends over the concentration range. Combined with the TEG parameters presented in Table 1, no abnormal values were seen in the coagulation parameters and all four traces nearly overlapped at 0.001 mg/mL, which was the safe concentration for all groups. When the concentration was 0.01 mg/mL, the coagulation parameters of K and α value appeared abnormal in a sample of the PEI/pDNA group, while both (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG groups were within the normal range, indicating that PEI/pDNA may affect the activity of fibrinogen polymerization. When the concentration increased to 0.1 mg/mL, the prolonged R value and abnormal K and α value were seen with all samples of the PEI/pDNA group, indicating that at this concentration, PEI/pDNA was beginning to affect the activity of the coagulation factors and fibrinogen polymerization. At the same concentration, abnormal K and α value were observed in the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group owing to its relatively lower positive charge. The abnormal MA value that appeared in the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group could be attributed to the attenuation of clot strength from DMMA-induced RBC rupture, which was found earlier. When the concentration reached 1 mg/mL, almost all values of three groups were abnormal. This result was consistent with the results of APTT and PT assays.

The TEG results indicate that PEI/pDNA can induce fibrin aggregation, prolong clotting time, and reduce clot strength in a concentration-dependent manner, and it likely exhibited an anticoagulant effect. Both the DMMA and mPEG modification can significantly expand the safe concentration range of the vector and help reduce its effect of PEI on coagulation function. The TEG results were consistent with the APTT results but contrary to the fibrin aggregation experiments. The difference in results of fibrin aggregation is related to the selected sample and method, as TEG uses whole blood as the sample and tests for change in mechanical strength, while the turbidimetry test uses pure fibrinogen solution for detecting variations in OD, which has also been reported in a previous work [44].

The complement system is part of the innate immune system [45] and is a group of more than 40 soluble cell surface proteins found in body fluids and cell surfaces that interact to recognize and condition foreign biological materials [46]. In addition, the complement system is the major pro-inflammatory system that plays a role upstream of leukocyte and cytokine responses [47]. Therefore, complement activation has become one of the indicators for evaluating the immunocompatibility of blood-contacting nanovectors. The central event of complement activation is the cleavage of the C3 protein into opsin C3b and anaphylatoxin C3a by pathogen recognition, which leads to activating the system in cascade [47,48]. Therefore, the degree of complement activation caused by a vector can be indicated by detecting the concentration of C3a in plasma.

As shown in Fig. 8 a, complement component C3a was activated by PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG in a concentration-dependent manner. Specifically, at a concentration of 0.01 mg/mL, the three vectors caused no significant complement activation compared to the negative control. When the concentration was above 0.1 mg/mL, both the PEI/pDNA and (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA groups significantly activated the complement system, whereas (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG caused the complement activation only at the concentration of 1 mg/mL. Unexpectedly, the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group exhibited the highest C3a expression from 0.1 to 1 mg/mL, which was even higher than that of PEI/pDNA. We speculated that the strongest complement activation induced by (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA may be attributed to the physicochemical property of the DMMA-modified polycation. The diverse surface property of nanomaterials led to different plasma protein-binding pattern [49]. Nanoparticles with amine groups and methoxy groups have been found to induce the highest and lowest complement activation, respectively [47,50]. Further, carboxylated nanoparticles exhibit a higher degree of C3b deposition and retention than the hydroxylated nanoparticles [46,50]. In addition, particles with surface charges (whether positive or negative) can activate complement, and negative charges are critical for activating the complement system, while uncharged particles do not activate the complement in vitro [46]. Moreover, it has been reported that hydrophobic surfaces activate the complement more efficiently than hydrophilic surfaces [36]. Therefore, we concluded that the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA could cause stronger complement activation owing to the negatively charged carboxyl and hydrophobic structures on the DMMA. This indicated that the charge-reversible modification with DMMA did not attenuate the complement activation as PEGylation did but, rather, strongly activated the complement system, with the associated risk of causing adverse immune responses.

In the preclinical study, the candidate drug induces an undesired immune response, which is considered as one of the main reasons preventing its continued development [51]. In response to foreign antigens, immune cells express Toll-like receptors (TLRs) on their surface, activate their signaling pathways by identifying and binding pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) on the surface of the corresponding pathogenic microorganisms, and induce the expression of immune effector molecules and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α) [52,53]. Although inflammation is essential for resolving infections, it can also be lethal to cells and tissues if not properly regulated. Unrestricted activation of the immune system can produce a harmful, chronic inflammatory environment that can lead to diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, multiple sclerosis, and Alzheimer's disease [54]. Among them, IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α are mainly produced by monocytes [55,56].

In our study, we examined the effects of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG complexes on the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines in vitro with human monocytic leukemia cell line (THP-1) and human plasma. PBS was used as a negative control and LPS (agonist of TLR4) was used as a positive control. The results of pro-inflammatory cytokine expression are shown in Fig. 8b. Overall, the expression of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α in THP-1 cells and human plasma induced by the three vector groups followed a similar trend. Among them, the PEI/pDNA group can significantly cause the highest expression of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α in both THP-1 cells and human plasma. It showed that polycation/gene complex can stimulate immune cells to secrete inflammatory factors. The expression of IL-1β and TNF-α in (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG groups was significantly lower than that of the PEI/pDNA group, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG induced the least pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, which was attributed to the charge-shielding effect of the hydrophilic mPEG chain that reduced the immunostimulation of the polycation. Surprisingly, the expression of IL-6 caused by (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA was comparable or even higher than that caused by PEI/pDNA. The amount of IL-6 in plasma is approximately 10 times greater than that in the supernatant of THP-1 cells. According to previous reports, the two main roles in the inflammatory process are the complement system and immune cells [54]. It has also been reported that membrane attack complex (MAC) deposition by complement activation activates the intracellular signaling pathway NF-κB to induce the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines [57]. Therefore, we speculated that (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA-induced complement activation further promotes plasma monocytes to secrete IL-6. In addition, by comparing the trend for secretion levels of inflammatory factors in monocyte supernatants and plasma, a large proportion of the inflammatory factors expressed in plasma may be derived from monocytes. While mPEG modification appears to alleviate the inflammatory effects caused by polycations, the charge-reversible modification was less of an improvement than mPEG modification and may even amplify the inflammation by activating the complement system. Therefore, the charge-reversible modification is not ideal in improving the immunocompatibility of the polycation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptomic profiles and signaling pathway analysis
ce:section.ce:para: As transcriptomes are associated with the genetic information of the genome and proteomic biological functions, transcriptome sequencing is more frequently used to efficiently characterize the molecular mechanisms involved in cell stimulus responses [58,59]. To evaluate the potential molecular mechanism of the vector-induced inflammatory response, the overall RNA-seq of THP-1 cells treated with PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG was analyzed. In this study, the screening threshold for the DEGs was set to |log2 (FoldChange)| > 1 with an adjusted p value of < 0.05 (Fig. 9 a). The Venn diagram demonstrated the number of DEGs in different sets, and the intersecting parts represented the same DEGs, including up/downregulated DEGs. The volcano plots show the number of DEGs in the PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG compared to that in controls. A high degree of separation represented greater differences in the expression between the two groups. As shown in Fig. 9a, 97 genes were downregulated and 438 genes were upregulated in the PEI/pDNA group, 10 genes were downregulated and 25 genes were upregulated in the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group, and 11 genes were downregulated and 19 genes were upregulated in the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group. The DEG results suggested that PEI/pDNA induced a mass of DEGs in monocytes, which can be markedly reduced after DMMA and mPEG modification.

GO enrichment analysis was further performed to elucidate the functional properties of the gene whose expression significantly changes when THP-1 cells are stimulated by the polycationic gene vector [58]. The GO term enrichment histogram of the DEGs is used to directly reflect BPs, cellular components (CC), and molecular function (MF) [60]. BP is a recognized series of events or MFs with well-defined start and endpoints that correlate with the function of integrated genes [58,61]. Therefore, the 20 most notable GO items selected in BP are shown in Fig. S2. From the GO items of the PEI/pDNA group, 73 DEGs related to immunity and inflammation were selected and then mapped onto a heatmap (Fig. 9b: red: up-regulation and blue: down-regulation). Among these, 24 genes were up-regulated and 2 genes were down-regulated in the DEGs involved in the inflammatory response, and 32 genes involved in cytokine-mediated signaling pathways were inhibited or induced. In the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group, fewer genes (e.g., WNT5A and CD80) were up-regulated. These are involved in multiple BP such as positive regulation of leukocyte activation, and the cytokine-mediated signaling pathway, etc. In the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group, a similar trend in lowering inflammatory response-related DEGs and their abnormal level could be found. In short, PEI/pDNA can significantly up-regulate or down-regulate a large number of genes involved in inflammatory cell activation and inflammatory cytokine secretion in THP-1 cells, while DMMA and mPEG modification can significantly reduce these effects on the DEGs. It was worth noting that DEGs in heatmap also confirmed that the up-regulated gene expression of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α (highlighted with red box in Fig. 9b) in PEI/pDNA-treated THP-1 cells is in agreement with the results of the pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion assay, where DMMA or mPEG modification helped to improve such gene up-regulation.

KEGG is a bioinformatics resource that links genomic information to higher order functional information to facilitate systematic analysis of gene function [62]. Pathway-based annotation and analysis help to further understand the biological function of genes [58,62]. In this work, KEGG analysis showed that unigenes in the PEI/pDNA group were involved in 186 pathways, while the unigenes in the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG groups were 25 and 7 pathways, respectively. According to previous reports, four signaling pathways, namely, MAP kinase (MAPK), nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB), Toll-like receptor (TLR), and Janus kinase/signal transducer and activator of transcription (JAK/STAT), have been shown to be involved in the activation of monocytes and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines [63–65]. Therefore, we further assessed whether or not the PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG elicited up/down-regulation of gene expression in these four pathways, and the results are shown in Fig. 9c. In the PEI/pDNA group, four genes (GF, IL1, TGFBR, and RasGRP) were up-regulated and one gene (CACN) was down-regulated in the MAPK signaling pathway; three genes (IL-1β, IL-8, and MIP-2) were up-regulated in the NF-κB signaling pathway, six genes (TLR1, TLR6, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and CD80) were up-regulated in the Toll-like receptor signaling pathway; and three genes (Cytokine, STAT, and PIAS) were up-regulated in the JAK-STAT signaling pathway. In the other two groups, only one gene (CD80) in the Toll-like receptor signaling pathway was up-regulated by the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group, and the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group did not influence the gene expression in the four inflammatory response-related signaling pathways.

The above profiling analysis allowed us to identify potential intermediaries of the immune inflammatory response to polycationic gene vectors. The results indicated that PEI/pDNA could elicit the greatest remarkable DEGs (most upregulated), some of which participated in regulating a broad range of immune BPs including leukocyte activation and inflammatory response, etc. This implies that PEI/pDNA may activate monocytes and induce a large number of immunoregulatory genes and variants. In contrast, DMMA or mPEG modification could alleviate such immunostimulatory effect by minimizing the DEGs. Furthermore, the PEI/pDNA group upregulated genes involved in the MAPK, NF-κB, Toll-like receptor, and JAK-STAT signaling pathways, which are associated with monocyte stimulation and inflammatory cytokine secretion, while the (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA group only upregulated the gene involved in the Toll-like receptor signaling pathway and the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group had no effect on the genes involved in the four signaling pathways. It suggested that polycation/gene complex activated monocytes to secrete proinflammatory cytokines possibly through these four signaling pathways, and DMMA or mPEG modification could effectively inhibit the activation of these signaling pathways. When combining the transcriptomic profiles with the results of complement activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion, we found that the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines was correlated with the number of altered genes. This further confirms that the abnormal pro-inflammatory cytokine expression from monocytes, stimulated by the PEI/pDNA complex, was mediated by inflammation-related gene alteration and the signaling pathways (MAPK, NF-κB, Toll-like receptor, and JAK-STAT) activation. The effects from DMMA or mPEG modification of the polycation are likely due to the reduction in endocytosis into THP-1 cells (Fig. S3). By comparison, PEGylated modification created a better immune evasion capacity than did the charge-reversible DMMA modification owing to its less distinct effect on complement and activation of the inflammatory signaling pathways.

In vitro coagulation is assessed in a relatively closed environment away from the exchanges within living organism, as well as foreign nanomaterials may be cleared by RES or MPS during blood circulation [66]. Therefore, an in vivo TEG experiment was performed using Wistar rats to elucidate the impacts of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG on the blood coagulation process. Representative TEG curves are shown in Fig. 10 a, and the clotting kinetics values are shown in Table 2 . Overall, the trends for the TEG traces obtained from intravenous administration of vectors appear similar to the in vitro results. Specifically, the R values in the presence of the three polycation/gene groups were prolonged compared to those of the saline control, which suggests that the R value seemed to be the most sensitive indicator for adverse effects of clot formation caused by polycation/gene complexes. It may be due to electrostatic interaction between the polycations and blood coagulation factors. As previously reported, cationic PAMAM can also extend the R value [17,36]. As expected, the PEI/pDNA group showed increased R and K values and a decreased α angle and MA values, implying PEI/pDNA delayed the initial fibrin formation, inhibited fibrin polymerization, and impaired clot strength in vivo. In contrast, the DMMA or mPEG modifications on PEI/pDNA indeed significantly improved in vivo blood compatibility, except for the abnormal R value.

The in vivo results further confirm the results of the TEG evaluation in vitro, indicating that the charge-reversal strategy can improve the effect of the polycationic gene vector on the blood coagulation process, and are comparable to those of the mPEG modification.

To comprehensively investigate the potential side effects of the PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, and (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG in vivo, we collected rat blood after 24 h of tail vein injection with one of the above groups for overall blood routine analysis using an automatic blood cell analyzer. As shown in Table 3 , intravenous injection of PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG solution resulted in dramatic increases in the number of inflammatory cells (i.e., WBCs and sub-types (i.e., lymphocytes, monocytes, and neutrophils)) compared to that in the saline control group. As a possible explanation, activation of the innate immune system such as the complement system or monocytes by the polycation/gene complex would induce an inflammatory response and recruit more immune cells [67]. The inflammation-induced increase in WBCs, particularly monocytes in vivo, also supports the in vitro activation of the inflammatory response caused by polycation/gene complex.

We further measured the expression level of pro-inflammatory cytokines in rat serum after intravenous administration of (PEI/pDNA, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, or (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG), and the results are shown in Fig. 10b. The trend of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α expression levels in vivo induced by these three groups was approximately similar to the in vitro results. More specifically, IL-1β secretion was significantly elevated only in the PEI/pDNA group in vivo. The TNF-α expression level significantly increased from PEI/pDNA or (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA, while the expression level of the (PEI/pDNA)-mPEG group showed almost no difference compared with that of the physiological saline control group. Similarly, the three groups significantly induced high expression of IL-6 in vivo. The in vivo results validated the in vitro data, demonstrating that polycationic gene vector can induce an inflammatory response in blood and may increase the risk for multiorgan failure [68]. It also indicated that the charge-reversal strategy based on DMMA modification is inferior to mPEG modification from the standpoint of improving the inflammatory response caused by polycationic gene vector.

In this work, we mapped the biological effects of charge-reversible polycationic gene vector based on DMMA modified PEI/pDNA complex on blood components, coagulation function, and immune response at cellular and molecular levels as compared to conventional PEGylated modification. Overall, charge-reversible modification showed better performance in improving the hemocompatibility even higher than PEGylation but was inferior to mPEG modification in immunocompatibility (Table 4 ). Specifically, it was found that charge-reversible (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA could reduce nonspecific endocytosis by vascular endothelial cells, prevent platelet activation, weaken the effect on activity of the clotting factors, alleviate the abnormal fibrinogen polymerization, and help to improve the impairment of the blood clot in the whole dynamic coagulation process caused by polycation/gene complex. However, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA caused the RBC morphological alternation and lead to hemolysis through impairing on cytoskeleton. On the other hand, (PEI/pDNA)-DMMA alleviated the polycation-induced pro-inflammatory cytokine overexpression, whereas it induced the highest level of complement activation even higher than that of the PEI/pDNA group. Transcriptome sequencing results demonstrated that abnormal pro-inflammatory cytokine expression was correlated with the number of DEGs and mediated by inflammation-related signaling pathways (MAPK, NF-κB, Toll-like receptor, and JAK-STAT) activation. In vivo experiments further confirmed the in vitro results. These findings provide more comprehensive information (from blood compatibility to immune compatibility) for developing charge-reversal-based delivery systems from laboratory to clinic.
